articles.title: End of heatwave confirmed with record still unmatched - RTE.ie
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
The weather station in Moorepark, Co Cork, has recorded a provisional temperature of 24.9C today, meaning this spell of warm weather has not equalled the longest heatwave on record in Ireland.
Met Éireann previously said there was a possibility that the longest heatwave on record in Ireland, of 14 days in August 1976, could be matched today.
The longest heatwave recorded was 14 days from 14-27 August 1976 and is jointly held at two stations - one in Ballybrittas, Co Laois, and at Birr, Co Offaly.
A heatwave is technically at least five consecutive days of 25C and above.
Meanwhile, a weather advisory for high temperatures is in place until Monday.
Met Éireann has said that while temperatures will fall back somewhat from recent high levels, impacts will still be felt from the prolonged spell of very warm or hot and dry weather.
They urged people to be careful around water with an increased use of waterways, lakes and beaches.
They also said that people may experience some heat stress and dehydration, especially the vulnerable and elderly. They advised people to be aware of animal welfare issues, as well as drought concerns, wildfires and forest fires.
Today it will be warm and dry for most with the sunniest conditions in the midlands, south and east, and highest temperatures of 23C to 26C. Cloudier in the north and northwest with highest temperatures there of 17C to 22C in a light to moderate northerly breeze.
Tonight it will be dry and largely clear with isolated mist patches developing. Fresher than recent nights with lowest temperatures of 9C to 13C degrees in a light northerly or variable breeze.
Tomorrow is forecast to be dry and clear in most places. It will be cloudier in Ulster and north Connacht with a few patches of drizzle. Lowest temperatures of 10C to 14C degrees in a light northerly breeze.
Monday will become cloudier overall, with sunny spells over the southern half of the country.
We need your consent to load this rte-player contentWe use rte-player to manage extra content that can set cookies on your device and collect data about your activity. Please review their details and accept them to load the content.Manage Preferences
Most places will remain dry but patches of drizzle will affect parts of Connacht, Ulster and north Leinster.
Warm in Leinster and Munster with highest temperatures of 20C to 25C degrees. Cooler in Ulster and Connacht with highest temperatures of 17C to 20C degrees in moderate northerly winds.
Hundreds of hectares of mountain land burned
Wicklow Fire Service says wild fires in the Glencree/Featherbed mountain area on the Wicklow-Dublin border have burnt 300-400 hectares of mountain land.
Fire fighters were on scene again today and have extinguished all of the main fires, but are continuing to monitor the situation as some areas continue to smoulder.
Several helicopters were deployed to drop water on the affected areas.
Much of the area was drained and dried out when peat was extracted some years ago and this has left it vulnerable to fire, according to the Wicklow Fire Service.
While the immediate causes of the fires have not been established, fire fighters says it was due to human activity, possibly accidental.
Temperatures in the area are falling which may help control the fires, but rising winds are also causing concern.
Spain braces for temperatures in excess of 40C
Meanwhile, Spain is set to enter its third heatwave of the summer on Tuesday, with temperatures forecast to climb above 40C across large parts of the country, the national weather office said.
The heatwave will be fuelled by a persistent area of high pressure that will trap hot, dry air from North Africa over Spain for several days, pushing temperatures to exceptionally high levels, AEMET said in a statement.
The heat is expected to intensify through Thursday, which forecasters say is likely to be the peak of the event, with some isolated areas potentially exceeding 45C.
"The level of danger will be significant during the central hours of the day, especially for outdoor activities and vulnerable people," the weather agency said.
Spain has already endured two other heatwaves this summer - one in late June that broke temperature records across Europe, and another in early July.
A ferocious blaze in the southeastern province of Almeria earlier this month killed 13 people, making it Spain's deadliest wildfire in recent years.
Additional reporting: Éilís Sheehy, Colman O'Sullivan

articles.title: PJM Issued First Backup-Generator Warnings During Heat Wave - Data Center Knowledge

articles.full_text:
PJM Issued First Backup-Generator Warnings During Heat Wave
The new warnings process saw its first operational use as PJM neared record demand, but the grid operator says no large-load backup generators were called to run.
PJM issued “Transmission Security – Emergency Use of Backup-Generator Warning” during this month’s record-setting heat wave, but ultimately did not call on large-load customers to operate their backup generators, the grid operator confirmed.
The warnings were sent on July 2 in the Mid-Atlantic and Dominion regions and again on July 3 in Dominion, according to a July 9 presentation to PJM’s Operating Committee. The alerts followed a US Department of Energy (DOE) emergency order under Section 202(c) that allowed certain large-load customers to shift to on-site generation if needed to protect grid reliability.
PJM now says the warnings were precautionary. The grid operator never escalated to an “Emergency Use of Backup-Generators Action,” meaning no customer-owned backup generators were dispatched under the new procedure.
For operators, the episode offers the first real-world test of a new reliability tool designed to tap into large-load backup generation during grid emergencies. Although the procedure was not fully activated, the warnings demonstrate that PJM was prepared to use it as electricity demand approached record levels.
First Use of PJM’s Backup-Generator Warning
PJM added the emergency backup-generator procedures to its operating manuals this year amid growing reliability concerns and the DOE’s 202(c) emergency order. Under the process, transmission owners report how much large-load demand could be transferred to backup generation within 15 minutes if PJM requests action during an emergency.
In its July 9 Operating Committee presentation, PJM placed the backup-generator warnings among a series of measures deployed as temperatures climbed across the region. Those actions included DOE coordination beginning June 26, North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) Energy Emergency Alerts, maintenance recalls, maximum generation actions, heavy-load voltage procedures, and transmission loading relief.
PJM reported a served peak load of 162,713 MW during the event. Accounting for emergency actions and customer load reductions, PJM estimated that unrestricted demand reached 168,158 MW, which the grid operator identified as an all-time unrestricted peak.
Joseph Bowring, president of Monitoring Analytics, PJM’s Independent Market Monitor, characterized the warnings as “precautionary.”
“[The] actual extent of potential reliability contribution is not well known, in my opinion,” Bowring told Data Center Knowledge. “The significance is that PJM was getting very close to a shortage of primary reserves.”
Former Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) Chairman Neil Chatterjee said the episode reflects an evolving posture toward customer-owned generation. “This is not extraordinary and should not be considered rare from this point forward,” he told Data Center Knowledge. “This is more a bellwether, ‘canary in the substation,’ if you will.”
Chatterjee added that grid operators and utilities are both programmatically and reactively relying on dispatchable distributed energy resources to mitigate grid stress and larger power emergencies. As those resources continue proving their value, he said, their use is likely to become more common.
No Dispatch Ordered
In response to questions from Data Center Knowledge, PJM confirmed that it did not issue an Emergency Use of Backup Generators Action during the July 2–3 period. “No such action was required,” a PJM spokesperson said. The operator said no backup generators were operated in response to the warnings and no large-load backup generation was dispatched under the emergency procedure.
PJM also clarified that the warning was issued to the Dominion control zone.
While all PJM transmission owners reported the amount of large-load demand that could be transferred to backup generation within 15 minutes, PJM declined to disclose the total megawatts available. The operator said it does not collect data on the number of eligible facilities, nor does it estimate how much of the available backup generation comes from data centers versus other large commercial and industrial customers.
PJM said it currently follows the definition of “large loads” established in the DOE’s Section 202(c) order. The 50 MW threshold discussed in the stakeholder process has not yet been formally adopted into PJM’s governing documents.
A New Reliability Tool Among Many
The Operating Committee presentation shows that PJM relied on numerous reliability tools during the heat wave. Demand response programs reduced load by approximately 6,113 MW on July 2 and by 5,037 MW on July 3.
The DOE emergency emissions order covered 51 generating plants, 594 generating units, and approximately 20.4 GW of capacity. PJM reported that 17 plants, representing 94 generating units and about 3.25 GW of capacity, operated beyond normal emissions limits for a combined 1,107 operating hours.
PJM also reported three Shared Reserve Events, two Maximum Generation Emergency/Load Management Alerts, two NERC Energy Emergency Alert Level 1 declarations, and five Hot Weather Alerts. On June 29, PJM approved five shortage cases after “solar dropped out faster than steam could ramp up.”
Although PJM never called on large-load customers to switch to backup generation, the July heat wave marked the first operational use of the warning process, offering an early indication of how the grid operator may manage large flexible loads as electricity demand from data centers continues to grow.
